Checks if proxy is configured in common.PROXY_URL and if so, adds proxy configuration to both launch_args and context_args. Launches the appropriate browser (firefox, webkit, or chromium) using the playwright instance with launch_args. Creates a new browser context using self.browser.new_context(**context_args). Creates a new page in the context using self.context.new_page(). Returns the page object for use in test flows.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Performs complete cleanup of all Playwright resources in the correct order: page, context, browser, and playwright instance. First checks if self.page exists and attempts to close it using self.page.close(), catching and logging any exceptions. Then checks if self.context exists and attempts to close it using self.context.close(). After closing the context, if logging is enabled (GlobalState.collect_logs is truthy) and logs_path exists, it verifies that HAR and video files were successfully saved by listing files in the logs directory. If collect_har is enabled, it looks for .har files and logs success or warning. If capture_video is enabled, it looks for .webm files and logs success or warning. Then checks if self.browser exists and attempts to close it using self.browser.close(), catching and logging any exceptions. Finally checks if self.playwright exists and attempts to stop it using self.playwright.stop(), catching and logging any exceptions. All exceptions during cleanup are caught and logged as warnings to prevent cleanup failures from crashing the test.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.docstring: Context manager entry
functions.what_it_does: Implements the context manager entry protocol by calling self.setup() and returning the result. This allows PlaywrightManager to be used with Python's 'with' statement for automatic resource management. When entering the context (with PlaywrightManager() as page:), this method is called and returns the Page object from setup().

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: exc_type
parameters.description: Exception type if an exception occurred in the context, None otherwise

parameters.name: exc_val
parameters.description: Exception value if an exception occurred in the context, None otherwise

parameters.name: exc_tb
parameters.description: Exception traceback if an exception occurred in the context, None otherwise

functions.docstring: Context manager exit
functions.what_it_does: Implements the context manager exit protocol by calling self.close() to clean up all Playwright resources. This method is automatically called when exiting the 'with' block, regardless of whether an exception occurred. The exc_type, exc_val, and exc_tb parameters contain exception information if an exception was raised in the context, but this method ignores them and always performs cleanup by calling close(). Does not suppress exceptions (returns None implicitly).
functions.usage_example:
with PlaywrightManager() as page:
    page.goto('https://example.com')
# __exit__ is called automatically here

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Converts a ReportStep instance to a dictionary representation suitable for JSON serialization and report generation. Uses the logged_message if available, otherwise falls back to the static pattern for display. Returns a dictionary with keys: id, file, line, pattern, status, message (the display message), timestamp, duration_ms, and first_timestamp (ISO format with Z suffix if first_timestamp_dt exists). This method is called when generating JSON reports to convert step objects to serializable dictionaries.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: reporter
parameters.description: ReportsManager instance that will receive step execution notifications

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the _LogHandler by calling the parent logging.Handler constructor with level set to logging.INFO, and stores the reporter reference in self.reporter. This handler will only process INFO-level messages and forward them to the reporter for step tracking.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: record
parameters.description: Log record containing the log message and metadata

functions.what_it_does: Overrides the logging.Handler emit method to intercept log records. Checks if the record level is INFO (record.levelno != logging.INFO returns early if not INFO). If it is INFO, extracts the message using record.getMessage() and calls self.reporter.record_step_execution(msg, record) to notify the reporter of the step execution. Wraps the entire logic in a try-except block that silently ignores any exceptions to prevent logging failures from breaking test execution.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by logging framework when framework_logger.info() is invoked

functions.method_signature: def __init__(self, flow_name: str, run_id: str, output_dir: Optional[str]):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow being executed

parameters.name: run_id
parameters.description: Unique identifier for this test run

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Directory where reports will be saved. Must not be None.

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the ReportsManager with flow name, run ID, and output directory. Raises ValueError if output_dir is None. Creates the output directory if it doesn't exist using os.makedirs with exist_ok=True. Initializes empty lists for self.steps (will hold ReportStep objects) and self._executed_step_ids (tracks which steps have been executed). Sets self._handler to None (will hold the log handler). Initializes empty dictionaries for self._built_index (pattern prefix to step IDs mapping), self._exact_pattern_index (exact pattern to step ID mapping), and self._location_index (file path and line number to step IDs mapping). Records the suite start time in self._suite_start as datetime.datetime.utcnow(). Initializes self._last_step_end_monotonic to None for duration tracking. Creates a StringIO buffer in self.console_buffer for capturing console output. Initializes stdout/stderr capture variables to None. Sets self._flow_dir to the test flow directory path. Initializes self._dynamic_added as an empty set and self._next_exec_order to 1 for tracking execution order.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Starts the reporting system by scanning source code for expected steps and installing log capture handlers. First calls self.scan_sources() to parse test flow Python files and extract all framework_logger.info() calls as potential steps. Creates a _LogHandler instance with self as the reporter and adds it to framework_logger using framework_logger.addHandler(). Creates a generic logging.Handler for capturing all log output (not just INFO), sets its level to DEBUG, applies a formatter with timestamp and level, and defines a custom emit function that writes formatted log records to self.console_buffer. Adds this handler to framework_logger. Attempts to redirect stdout and stderr by saving originals in self._orig_stdout and self._orig_stderr, creating _Tee wrappers that duplicate output to both the original streams and console_buffer, and assigning the tee objects to sys.stdout and sys.stderr. If stdout/stderr redirection fails, logs a debug message but continues. Logs a debug message indicating initialization is complete with the number of potential steps found and the report output path.

functions.method_signature: def finalize(self, success: bool, exception: Optional[BaseException] = None):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: success
parameters.description: Whether the test flow completed successfully

parameters.name: exception
parameters.description: Exception that caused test failure, if any

functions.what_it_does: Finalizes the reporting system by marking incomplete steps, removing handlers, restoring stdout/stderr, and generating reports. If success is False and there are executed steps, gets the last executed step and if its status is PASS or PENDING, changes it to FAIL. If an exception is provided, appends exception information to the step's logged_message (truncated to 500 characters). Iterates through all steps and changes any with status PENDING to NOT_EXECUTED. Removes the _LogHandler from framework_logger if it exists. Removes the log capture handler from framework_logger if it exists. Restores sys.stdout and sys.stderr to their original values if they were redirected. If ENABLE_HTML is True, calls self._generate_html() to create the HTML report. If ENABLE_JSON is True, calls self._write_json() to create the JSON report. Logs an info message with the report path (or indicates HTML is disabled if ENABLE_HTML is False).

functions.method_signature: def record_step_execution(self, log_message: str, record: logging.LogRecord):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: log_message
parameters.description: The log message text from framework_logger.info()

parameters.name: record
parameters.description: The logging.LogRecord containing metadata like pathname and lineno

functions.what_it_does: Core method that matches logged messages to expected steps and tracks execution. Normalizes the log message by stripping whitespace. Extracts absolute file path and line number from the log record. Imports time module for monotonic timing. Checks if the message is an attempt-like message (contains ' - attempt ' or ends with ' successful') and if it exists in the exact pattern index - if so, increments occurrence count and updates timestamp, then returns. Checks if the file path and line number exist in the location index - if so, iterates through candidate steps at that location and checks if the pattern matches using exact match or self._match() - if a match is found, calls self._mark_step_execution() and returns. Iterates through all PENDING steps looking for a pattern match - if found and it's an ellipsis pattern, verifies the prefix matches to avoid false positives - if valid, adds the location to the index, calls _mark_step_execution(), and returns. Iterates through all steps (not just PENDING) looking for pattern matches to handle retry scenarios - if found, increments occurrence count and updates timestamp. If no match is found and the log came from a file with a line number, attempts to extract the logger call pattern from the source code using AST parsing via self._extract_logger_call_pattern() - if successful, creates a new dynamic ReportStep, adds it to self.steps, updates indexes, marks it as executed, and returns. If still no match and it's an attempt-like message, creates a new dynamic step without source location. This complex logic handles pre-scanned steps, dynamic steps, retries, and location-based matching.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by _LogHandler.emit() when framework_logger.info() is invoked

functions.method_signature: def _mark_step_execution(self, step: ReportStep, normalized_msg: str, time_module):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The ReportStep object to mark as executed

parameters.name: normalized_msg
parameters.description: The normalized log message text

parameters.name: time_module
parameters.description: The time module for monotonic timing

functions.what_it_does: Marks a step as executed by updating its status, timestamp, execution order, and duration. Gets the current datetime as now_dt using datetime.datetime.utcnow() and formats it as ISO string with microseconds and Z suffix. Gets the current monotonic time as now_monotonic using time_module.monotonic(). If there are previously executed steps, gets the last executed step and if it has a start monotonic time but no duration, calculates the duration as the difference between now_monotonic and the previous step's start time in milliseconds. If the step's execution_order is None (first time executing), assigns it the next execution order number from self._next_exec_order, increments _next_exec_order, appends the step ID to _executed_step_ids, sets the step's _start_monotonic to now_monotonic, and if first_timestamp_dt is None, sets it to now_dt. Sets the step status to PASS. If the step doesn't have a logged_message yet, sets it to normalized_msg. Updates the step's timestamp to now_iso. Increments the step's occurrences count (or sets it to 1 if it was 0).

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Scans test flow source code files to extract expected test steps by parsing framework_logger.info() calls using Python AST. Creates a search_paths list containing self._flow_dir (the test flow directory). Initializes step_id to 1. Iterates through each path in search_paths - if the path is not a directory, skips it. Walks the directory tree using os.walk() to find all files. For each file, checks if it ends with .py - if not, skips it. Constructs the full file path and attempts to read the file content and parse it using ast.parse() to get an AST tree. If parsing fails, silently continues to the next file. Walks the AST tree using ast.walk() to find all nodes. For each node, checks if it's an ast.Call node (function call). Checks if the function being called is an attribute access (ast.Attribute) where the object is 'framework_logger' and the attribute is 'info'. If so, checks if there are arguments to the call. Extracts the pattern from the first argument using self._extract_pattern(). If a pattern is extracted, creates a ReportStep with the step_id, relative file path, line number from the node, and the extracted pattern. Appends the step to self.steps. Adds the step ID to self._built_index using the first 10 characters of the pattern (lowercased) as the key. Increments step_id. This pre-scanning allows the reporter to know what steps to expect before execution begins.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: step_id
parameters.description: The step ID (1-based index)

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves a ReportStep object by its ID. Since step IDs are 1-based but the list is 0-indexed, returns self.steps[step_id - 1]. This is a simple accessor method used throughout the class to get step objects by ID.

parameters.name: node
parameters.description: AST node representing the argument to framework_logger.info()

functions.what_it_does: Extracts the log message pattern from an AST node representing the argument to framework_logger.info(). If the node is an ast.Constant with a string value, returns the string value directly (this handles simple string literals like 'Login successful'). If the node is an ast.JoinedStr (f-string), iterates through the values in the f-string - for each value, if it's a Constant string, appends it to parts, otherwise appends the placeholder '{…}' (ellipsis in braces) to represent the interpolated expression. Joins all parts and returns the pattern string with placeholders. If the node is neither Constant nor JoinedStr, returns None. This allows the reporter to match f-strings with variable interpolation by using the ellipsis placeholder.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: The expected pattern (may contain ellipsis placeholders)

parameters.name: message
parameters.description: The actual logged message

functions.what_it_does: Determines if a logged message matches an expected pattern. First checks for exact match - if pattern equals message, returns True. If both pattern and message contain ' - attempt ' (retry pattern), extracts the action part before ' - attempt ' from both, removes ellipsis placeholders from the pattern action, and checks if the pattern action is contained in the message action - if so, returns True. Creates a simplified pattern by removing all ellipsis placeholders and stripping whitespace. If the simplified pattern is non-empty and is contained in the message, returns True. Otherwise returns False. This flexible matching allows patterns with f-string interpolation to match actual logged messages.

functions.method_signature: def _extract_logger_call_pattern(self, file_path: str, lineno: int) -> Optional[str]:

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: Absolute path to the source file

parameters.name: lineno
parameters.description: Line number where the logger call occurred

functions.what_it_does: Attempts to extract the logger call pattern from a specific line in a source file by parsing the file with AST. Opens the file at file_path, reads the source code, and parses it using ast.parse(). Walks the AST tree looking for ast.Call nodes where the line number matches lineno. For each matching call, checks if the function is an attribute access with attr 'info' and the value is a Name node with id 'framework_logger'. If so, checks if there are arguments and calls self._extract_pattern() on the first argument to extract the pattern. Returns the extracted pattern if found. If any exception occurs during file reading or parsing, returns None. If no matching logger call is found at that line, returns None. This is used to dynamically discover steps that were not pre-scanned (e.g., steps in dynamically loaded modules).

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Property that returns the full path to the HTML report file. Constructs the path by joining self.output_dir with 'report.html' using os.path.join(). This property provides a consistent way to access the report path throughout the class.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Generates a comprehensive HTML report with step-by-step execution details, color-coded status, duration metrics, and embedded console logs. Calculates summary statistics: total steps, passed steps, failed steps, and not executed steps. Gets the current UTC timestamp. Retrieves console output and metadata using self._get_console_output(). Defines an escape function using html.escape() for HTML safety. Initializes an empty list for HTML table rows. Defines a status_color dictionary mapping step statuses to background colors. Separates steps into executed steps (those with execution_order) and pending steps (those without). Sorts executed steps by first_timestamp_dt and execution_order to show them in chronological order. Iterates through executed steps and calculates duration for steps that don't have duration_ms by computing the time difference to the next step. Creates an execution display index mapping step IDs to their display numbers (1, 2, 3...). Iterates through all steps (executed first, then pending) and generates HTML table rows with color-coded backgrounds, displaying execution number, status, message, file location, timestamp, duration, and occurrence count. Constructs a complete HTML document with embedded CSS for styling (sticky headers, color-coded badges, responsive table, collapsible console log section) and JavaScript for client-side filtering. Includes a summary section with total/pass/fail/not-executed counts as colored badges and a filter input box. Embeds the console output in a collapsible details element with a pre tag. Writes the complete HTML document to self.report_path. If writing fails, logs an error.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by finalize() if ENABLE_HTML is True

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Generates a JSON companion report containing all step data and console output in a machine-readable format. Constructs the JSON file path by joining self.output_dir with 'report.json'. Retrieves console output and metadata using self._get_console_output(). Opens the JSON file for writing and uses json.dump() to write a dictionary containing: flow name, run_id, generated UTC timestamp, steps array (each step converted to dictionary using step.to_row()), and console object with truncated flag, byte count, line count, and full content. Uses indent=2 for readable formatting. If any exception occurs during JSON writing, logs a warning but does not fail the test.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by finalize() if ENABLE_JSON is True

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves the captured console output from the StringIO buffer and applies size-based truncation if needed. Gets the raw string value from self.console_buffer using getvalue(). Initializes truncated flag to False. Encodes the raw string to UTF-8 bytes to check size. If the byte size exceeds CONSOLE_MAX_BYTES (2MB), performs intelligent truncation by keeping the first 70% and last 25% of the content with a '...<TRUNCATED>...' marker in between. Decodes the truncated bytes back to string using UTF-8 with error handling (errors='ignore'). Sets truncated flag to True. Counts the number of lines in the output by counting newline characters plus 1 if the string doesn't end with a newline. Returns a tuple of (output_string, metadata_dict) where metadata contains truncated flag, byte count, and line count. This prevents extremely large console outputs from bloating the HTML report.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: Data to write to both streams

functions.what_it_does: Writes data to both the original stream and the buffer. Attempts to write data to self.original using original.write(data), catching and ignoring any exceptions. Attempts to write data to self.buf using buf.write(data), catching and ignoring any exceptions. This dual-write behavior implements the tee functionality, duplicating output to both destinations.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically when sys.stdout.write() is invoked after redirection

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Flushes the original stream to ensure buffered data is written. Attempts to call self.original.flush(), catching and ignoring any exceptions. Does not flush the StringIO buffer as it doesn't require flushing. This ensures that output to the original stdout/stderr is not delayed by buffering.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically when sys.stdout.flush() is invoked after redirection

parameters.name: tag
parameters.description: HTML tag name of the element

parameters.name: href
parameters.description: href attribute value of the element

parameters.name: onclick
parameters.description: onclick attribute value of the element

functions.what_it_does: Determines the semantic type of an HTML element based on its tag name and attributes for categorization in the scraping output. Converts tag to uppercase and checks various conditions: if tag starts with 'H' and is 2 characters long with a digit as the second character (H1-H9), returns 'Heading'. If tag is 'A' and href is truthy, returns 'Link'. If tag is 'BUTTON' or onclick is truthy, returns 'Button'. If tag is INPUT, SELECT, or TEXTAREA, returns 'FormControl'. If tag is LABEL, returns 'Label'. If tag is IMG, returns 'Image'. Otherwise returns the tag name in title case. This semantic classification helps organize scraped elements by their functional role rather than just their HTML tag.

functions.method_signature:
def scrape_context(
    flow_name,
    context_name,
    page,
    excel_path,
    output_dir,
    screenshot_only=False,
    sub_context=None,
    lang_code=None,
    animations=False,
    target_dir=None
):

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow being scraped

parameters.name: context_name
parameters.description: Name of the context/page being scraped

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object representing the page to scrape

parameters.name: excel_path
parameters.description: Path to the Excel workbook where scraped data will be written

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Directory where screenshots and animations will be saved

parameters.name: screenshot_only
parameters.description: If True, only captures full-page screenshot without scraping elements

parameters.name: sub_context
parameters.description: Optional sub-context name for organizing scraped data

parameters.name: lang_code
parameters.description: Language code for naming screenshot files

parameters.name: animations
parameters.description: If True, extracts animated SVGs, GIFs, and visual animations

parameters.name: target_dir
parameters.description: Optional target directory (currently unused in implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Main scraping function that extracts all UI elements and visual content from a web page and writes the data to an Excel workbook. Gets the current timestamp formatted as 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'. Creates a screenshots directory inside output_dir using ensure_dirs(). Constructs a subcontext suffix string if sub_context is provided. Captures a full-page screenshot using save_full_page_screenshot() with the flow name, context name with suffix, and screenshot directory. Initializes an empty elements list and a seen set for deduplication. Adds a FullPage row to elements with the screenshot filename. If screenshot_only is True, loads or creates the Excel workbook, writes the single FullPage row, logs a message, and returns early. Otherwise, queries all elements in the page body using page.query_selector_all('body *'). For each element handle, gets the tag name and skips if it's in Constants.IGNORE_TAGS or not visible. Gets the inner text and skips if empty. Gets the bounding box and skips if it's None or has zero width/height. Gets href and onclick attributes. Determines the semantic type using get_semantic_type(). Creates a deduplication key from sub_context, text, tag, and href. If the key is already in the seen set, skips this element. Adds the key to seen. Constructs a screenshot filename with context, subcontext, language code, index, semantic type, and text hash. Takes an element screenshot using take_element_handle_screenshot(). Appends a row to elements with all the element data including Context, SubContext, PageURL, Timestamp, Type, Tag, Text, Href, Screenshot filename, and empty ExcludeRegions. Catches and logs exceptions for elements that fail to scrape (except for 'Node is not an HTMLElement' errors which are silently ignored). If animations is True, creates an animations directory, extracts animated SVGs using extract_animated_svgs() and for each SVG, saves the SVG XML file and/or GIF file (controlled by SAVE_ONLY_GIF_FROM_SVG toggle), and adds rows to elements. Extracts animated GIFs using extract_animated_gifs() and for each GIF, saves the GIF file and adds a row to elements. Extracts visual animations using extract_animated_visuals() with selector '.stepsImage' and for each visual animation, adds a row to elements. Finally, loads or creates the Excel workbook, writes all elements to the workbook using write_excel_context(), and logs the number of elements scraped.
functions.usage_example:
scrape_context(
    flow_name='login_flow',
    context_name='login_page',
    page=playwright_page,
    excel_path='/path/to/baseline.xlsx',
    output_dir='/path/to/output',
    screenshot_only=False,
    sub_context='main_form',
    lang_code='en-US',
    animations=True
)

parameters.name: level
parameters.description: Logging level constant from logging module (e.g., logging.DEBUG, logging.INFO)

functions.what_it_does: Updates the logging level for the framework logger and all its handlers. Sets the global _LOG_LEVEL variable to the provided level. Calls framework_logger.setLevel(level) to update the logger's level. Iterates through all handlers attached to framework_logger and calls handler.setLevel(level) on each to ensure all handlers respect the new level. This function is called from aura.py when the --debug flag is provided to enable debug-level logging.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Logger name

parameters.name: log_file
parameters.description: Log file name

parameters.name: max_bytes
parameters.description: Maximum log file size in bytes before rotation (default 5MB)

parameters.name: backup_count
parameters.description: Number of backup log files to keep

functions.what_it_does: Creates and configures the framework logger with both file and console handlers. Gets or creates a logger with the specified name using logging.getLogger(). Sets the logger level to _LOG_LEVEL (defaults to INFO). Sets propagate to False to prevent duplicate logging. Checks if the logger already has handlers - if not, creates a formatter with timestamp, level, and message. Constructs the log file path by joining Constants.LOG_DIR with the log_file name. Creates a RotatingFileHandler with the file path, max_bytes size limit, backup_count for rotation, and UTF-8 encoding. Sets the file handler level to _LOG_LEVEL and applies the formatter. Adds the file handler to the logger. Creates a StreamHandler for console output, sets its level to _LOG_LEVEL, applies the formatter, and adds it to the logger. Returns the configured logger. The RotatingFileHandler automatically rotates log files when they reach max_bytes, keeping backup_count old files.

parameters.name: cls
parameters.description: Class reference

functions.what_it_does: Resets all GlobalState class attributes to their default values (None, False, or empty dict). Sets stack, locale, language, printer_profile, easy_enroll, biz_model, region, country, country_code, language_code, address_payload, selected_printer_sku, tenant_email, flow_name, timestamp, mode, run_dir, target, payment_method, and simulator_platform to None. Sets headless and collect_logs to False. Sets requirements to an empty dictionary. This method is called at the beginning of Config.init() to ensure a clean state before loading new configuration.

parameters.name: cls
parameters.description: Class reference

parameters.name: kwargs
parameters.description: Keyword arguments containing configuration values from command-line or test setup

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the framework configuration by resetting GlobalState and populating it with provided values and loaded data. Calls GlobalState.reset() to clear any previous state. Extracts specific keys from kwargs and assigns them to GlobalState attributes: stack, locale, language, printer_profile (from 'profile' key), easy_enroll, biz_model (defaults to 'Flex'), headless (defaults to False), mode, flow_name, timestamp, run_dir, target, payment_method (defaults to 'credit_card_master'), simulator_platform, and requirements (defaults to empty dict). Calls cls._load_locale_info() to load locale data from locale_map.json and populate country, country_code, region, language, and language_code in GlobalState. Calls cls._load_address_data() to load address data from address_data.json and populate address_payload in GlobalState. Calls cls._resolve_printer_sku() to load printer profiles from printer_profiles.json and populate selected_printer_sku in GlobalState based on region and easy_enroll settings. Iterates through all remaining kwargs and sets them as attributes on GlobalState using setattr(). This method is the main entry point for framework configuration and is called from aura.py with command-line arguments.
functions.usage_example: Config.init(stack='prod', locale='US', language='en', profile='OfficeJet', headless=True, mode='validate', flow_name='login_flow')

functions.what_it_does: Loads locale information from locale_map.json and populates GlobalState with country, country_code, region, language, and language_code. Opens Constants.LOCALE_MAP_PATH file and loads the JSON data. Checks if GlobalState.locale exists in the data - if not, raises ValueError with a descriptive message. Retrieves the locale data dictionary for the specified locale. Extracts and assigns country, code (as country_code), and region to GlobalState. Extracts the languages array from locale data. If GlobalState.language is not set, uses the first language from the languages array. Constructs language_code by combining language and country_code with a hyphen (e.g., 'en-US') and assigns it to GlobalState. This method is called during Config.init() to resolve locale-specific configuration.

Loads address data from address_data.json and populates GlobalState.address_payload with the address for the configured country. Opens Constants.ADDRESS_DATA_PATH file and loads the JSON data. Checks if GlobalState.country exists in the data - if not, raises ValueError with a descriptive message. Retrieves the address payload dictionary for the specified country and assigns it to GlobalState.address_payload. This address data is used by test flows to fill address forms during checkout or account creation. This method is called during Config.init() after locale information is loaded.

Resolves the printer SKU to use based on printer profile, region, and easy_enroll settings by loading data from printer_profiles.json. If GlobalState.printer_profile is not set, returns early without doing anything. Opens Constants.PRINTER_PROFILES_PATH file and loads the JSON data. Gets the profile name from GlobalState.printer_profile. Checks if the profile exists in the data - if not, raises ValueError. Retrieves the SKUs dictionary for the profile and gets the SKU list for GlobalState.region. If no SKUs are found for the region, raises ValueError. If GlobalState.easy_enroll is not None, converts it to a boolean by checking if the lowercase string equals 'yes'. Filters the SKU list to only include SKUs where the easy_enroll field matches the desired value. If no SKUs match the easy_enroll filter, raises ValueError. Selects the first SKU from the filtered list and assigns its 'sku' value to GlobalState.selected_printer_sku. This method is called during Config.init() after locale and address data are loaded.

Lambda function that returns the current value of GlobalState.headless. Provides a convenient way to check if headless mode is enabled without directly accessing GlobalState.headless. Returns True if headless mode is enabled, False otherwise.
functions.usage_example:
if headless_on():
    print('Running in headless mode')

functions.what_it_does: Generates and returns the current timestamp as a formatted string in the pattern YYYY_MM_DD_HH_MM_SS. Uses datetime.now() to get the current date and time, then formats it using strftime with the format '%Y_%m_%d_%H_%M_%S'. This timestamp format is used throughout the framework for naming output files, directories, and run identifiers to ensure uniqueness and chronological ordering.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The filename string to sanitize by removing invalid characters

functions.what_it_does: Sanitizes a filename string by replacing all invalid filesystem characters with underscores. Uses a regular expression pattern to match characters that are not allowed in filenames on Windows and other operating systems: backslash, forward slash, asterisk, question mark, colon, double quote, less than, greater than, and pipe. Each matched character is replaced with an underscore using re.sub(). Returns the sanitized filename string safe for use in file operations.

parameters.name: length
parameters.description: The length of the random string to generate. Defaults to 6 characters.

functions.what_it_does: Generates a random alphanumeric string of specified length using lowercase letters and digits. Creates a character pool by concatenating string.ascii_lowercase (a-z) and string.digits (0-9). Uses random.choices() to randomly select characters from this pool k times where k equals the length parameter. Joins the selected characters into a single string and returns it. Useful for generating unique identifiers, test data, or temporary names.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: The text to hash. Can be a string or bytes object.

functions.what_it_does: Generates a SHA256 hash of the provided text and returns the first 12 characters of the hexadecimal digest. If the input is already bytes, uses it directly; otherwise converts the text to bytes using UTF-8 encoding (or empty string if text is None). Computes the SHA256 hash using hashlib.sha256(), converts to hexadecimal string with hexdigest(), and slices the first 12 characters. This shortened hash is used for generating unique but readable identifiers for files and elements.
functions.usage_example: file_hash = hash_text('some content')  # Returns '3a5f8c2b9d1e' (first 12 chars of hash)

parameters.name: dirs
parameters.description: Variable number of directory paths to create

functions.what_it_does: Creates multiple directories if they do not already exist. Accepts a variable number of directory path strings as arguments. Iterates through each provided directory path and calls os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True, which creates the directory and any necessary parent directories without raising an error if the directory already exists. This is a convenience function for ensuring output directories exist before writing files.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name being accessed

functions.what_it_does: Magic method that enables attribute-style access to dictionary keys in the ElementsDict class. When an attribute is accessed using dot notation (e.g., obj.key), this method is called. It attempts to return the value associated with the key from the dictionary using self[name]. If the key does not exist in the dictionary, a KeyError is caught and re-raised as an AttributeError with a descriptive message indicating that the ElementsDict object has no such attribute.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name being set

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to assign to the attribute

functions.what_it_does: Magic method that enables setting dictionary keys using attribute-style assignment in the ElementsDict class. When an attribute is assigned using dot notation (e.g., obj.key = value), this method is called. It stores the value in the dictionary using self[name] = value, effectively treating attribute assignment as dictionary key assignment. This allows seamless use of both dictionary and attribute syntax on the same object.

functions.method_signature: def get_or_create_sheet(workbook, sheet_name: str, headers: list = None)

parameters.name: workbook
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: The name of the sheet to get or create

parameters.name: headers
parameters.description: Optional list of header strings. If None, uses Constants.SCRAPE_HEADERS.

functions.docstring: Return the sheet by name, or create it (with headers) if missing.
functions.what_it_does: Retrieves an existing Excel worksheet by name from a workbook, or creates a new one with headers if it does not exist. First checks if sheet_name exists in workbook.sheetnames. If it exists, returns the existing sheet. If not, creates a new sheet using workbook.create_sheet() with the specified title. Then writes header row by iterating through the headers list (or Constants.SCRAPE_HEADERS if headers is None), writing each header to the first row starting at column 1, and centering the text using Alignment(horizontal='center'). Returns the sheet object.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object to search

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: The selector value to search for in the first column

functions.docstring: Return True if any value in the first column matches 'selector'.
functions.what_it_does: Searches an Excel worksheet for a specific selector value in the first column and returns True if found, False otherwise. Iterates through all rows starting from row 2 (skipping header) using sheet.iter_rows() with min_row=2, max_col=1, and values_only=True to get only the first column values. For each row, unpacks the single cell value and compares it to the selector parameter. Returns True immediately upon finding a match, or False if no match is found after checking all rows. Used to prevent duplicate entries when scraping data.
functions.usage_example: exists = find_row(sheet, '[data-testid="login-btn"]')  # Returns True if selector exists

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object to append to

parameters.name: row_data
parameters.description: List of values to append as a new row. First element should be the selector.

functions.docstring: Append row_data to the sheet if not already present (by selector).
functions.what_it_does: Appends a row of scraped data to an Excel worksheet only if the selector (first element) is not already present and is not empty. Extracts the selector from row_data[0]. If the selector is empty or None, logs a debug message and returns without appending. Calls find_row() to check if the selector already exists in the sheet. If it exists, logs a debug message about skipping the duplicate and returns. If the selector is new, appends the entire row_data list to the sheet using sheet.append(). This prevents duplicate scraping of the same UI element.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object to adjust

functions.docstring: Resize columns based on maximum content length for readability.
functions.what_it_does: Automatically adjusts the width of all columns in an Excel worksheet based on the maximum content length in each column for better readability. Iterates through each column using sheet.columns. For each column, gets the column letter using get_column_letter() from the first cell's column index. Then iterates through all cells in that column, checking each cell's value. If the value is not None, calculates its string length and tracks the maximum length. After processing all cells in the column, sets the column width to max_len + 2 using sheet.column_dimensions[col_letter].width. The +2 adds padding for visual spacing.
functions.usage_example: auto_adjust_column_widths(sheet)  # Adjusts all column widths in the sheet

parameters.name: workbook
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: The name of the sheet to retrieve

functions.docstring: Return sheet by name or None (with a warning).
functions.what_it_does: Retrieves a worksheet by name from an Excel workbook, returning the sheet object if found or None with a warning if not found. First checks if the workbook exists and if sheet_name is in workbook.sheetnames. If both conditions are true, returns the sheet using workbook[sheet_name]. If the sheet is not found, logs a warning message using framework_logger.warning() indicating that the sheet was not found, and returns None. This provides safe sheet access with logging for debugging.
functions.usage_example: sheet = get_sheet_by_name(wb, 'LoginPage')  # Returns sheet or None

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to the Excel workbook

parameters.name: headers
parameters.description: List of header strings for new workbooks

functions.what_it_does: Loads an existing Excel workbook from the specified path or creates a new one if the file does not exist. First checks if the file exists using os.path.exists(). If it exists, loads the workbook using openpyxl.load_workbook() and gets the active worksheet. If the file does not exist, creates a new Workbook object, removes the default sheet that is automatically created, and sets ws to None. Returns a tuple of (workbook, worksheet). The headers parameter is provided for consistency but not used in this function - it would be used by calling code when creating sheets.

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object to save

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path where the workbook should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Saves an Excel workbook to the specified file path, creating the directory structure if it does not exist. Extracts the directory path from the full file path using os.path.dirname(). If the directory path is not empty, creates all necessary parent directories using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Then saves the workbook to the specified path using wb.save(). This ensures that the workbook can be saved even if the target directory does not exist yet.

parameters.name: excel_path
parameters.description: Path to the Excel file containing context data

functions.what_it_does: Extracts and returns a sorted list of unique context names from an Excel file. Loads the workbook using openpyxl.load_workbook() and gets the active worksheet. Creates an empty set called contexts to store unique context names. Iterates through all rows starting from row 2 (skipping header) using ws.iter_rows() with values_only=True. For each row, unpacks the first two values as flow and context (ignoring the rest with *_). If both flow and context are not empty, adds the context to the set. After processing all rows, converts the set to a sorted list and returns it. This is used to discover all unique test contexts in a test data file.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to check

functions.what_it_does: Checks if a Playwright element is hidden or disabled by evaluating JavaScript in the browser context. Uses elem.evaluate() to execute JavaScript that checks two conditions: whether el.offsetParent is null (which indicates the element is hidden via CSS display:none or visibility:hidden) or whether el.disabled is true (which indicates a form element is disabled). Returns True if either condition is met, False otherwise. This is more reliable than just checking visibility as it also detects CSS-hidden elements.
functions.usage_example: is_hidden = is_element_hidden_or_disabled(element_handle)  # Returns True if hidden or disabled

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to get position from

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves the position and dimensions of a Playwright element as a dictionary. Calls elem.bounding_box() to get the element's bounding box, which returns a dictionary with x, y, width, and height keys, or None if the element is not visible. Uses the or operator with an empty dict as fallback if bounding_box returns None. Constructs and returns a new dictionary with integer values for x, y, width, and height by using dict.get() with default value 0 for each key and converting to int. This ensures the function always returns a valid dictionary even if the element has no bounding box.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to extract styles from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts computed CSS styles from a Playwright element by executing JavaScript in the browser context. Uses elem.evaluate() to run a JavaScript function that gets the computed styles using window.getComputedStyle(el). Constructs and returns a dictionary containing font_family, font_size, font_weight, color, background (backgroundColor), and text_align properties. Each property uses the logical OR operator to return an empty string if the style property is null or undefined. This captures the actual rendered styles of the element as seen in the browser, not just the inline styles.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to extract text from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts the visible inner text from a Playwright element and returns it as a trimmed string. Calls elem.inner_text() to get the text content of the element as it appears to the user (excluding hidden elements and including line breaks). Then calls strip() to remove leading and trailing whitespace. Returns the cleaned text string. This is preferred over textContent for getting user-visible text as it respects CSS visibility and display properties.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Disables scrolling on a web page by injecting CSS that sets overflow to hidden on html and body elements. Calls page.add_style_tag() with content parameter containing CSS rule 'html, body { overflow: hidden !important; }'. The !important flag ensures this rule overrides any existing overflow styles. This is useful when capturing screenshots or scraping elements to prevent the page from scrolling during automation, which could cause elements to move or become obscured.
functions.usage_example: freeze_scroll(page)  # Disables scrolling on the page

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Re-enables scrolling on a web page by injecting CSS that sets overflow to auto on html and body elements. Calls page.add_style_tag() with content parameter containing CSS rule 'html, body { overflow: auto !important; }'. The !important flag ensures this rule overrides the previous freeze_scroll CSS. This restores normal scrolling behavior after it was disabled, allowing the page to scroll naturally again. Should be called after freeze_scroll when scrolling needs to be restored.
functions.usage_example: unfreeze_scroll(page)  # Re-enables scrolling on the page

functions.method_signature: def take_element_screenshot(page: Page, selector: str, output_path: str) -> bool

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: CSS selector or other locator string for the element

parameters.name: output_path
parameters.description: File path where the screenshot should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Captures a screenshot of a specific element on the page identified by a selector and saves it to the specified path. Wraps the operation in a try-except block. Locates the element using page.locator(selector). Checks if the element count is 0 or if the element is not visible using is_visible(), returning False if either condition is true. Extracts the directory path from output_path and creates it if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Calls element.screenshot(path=output_path) to capture and save the screenshot. Returns True on success. If any exception occurs, logs a warning with the selector and error message using framework_logger.warning() and returns False.

functions.method_signature: def take_element_handle_screenshot(handle: ElementHandle, output_path: str) -> bool

parameters.name: handle
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle object

parameters.name: output_path
parameters.description: File path where the screenshot should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Captures a screenshot of a specific element using an ElementHandle and saves it to the specified path. Wraps the operation in a try-except block. First checks if the element is visible using handle.is_visible(), returning False if not visible. Extracts the directory path from output_path and creates it if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Calls handle.screenshot(path=output_path) to capture and save the screenshot. Returns True on success. If any exception occurs, logs a warning with the error message using framework_logger.warning() and returns False. This is similar to take_element_screenshot but works with an already-obtained ElementHandle instead of a selector.

parameters.description: Name of the test context/checkpoint

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Optional output directory. If None, uses Constants.VALIDATE_OUTPUT_DIR.

functions.what_it_does: Captures a full-page screenshot and saves it with a timestamped filename. Determines the output directory using the provided output_dir parameter or Constants.VALIDATE_OUTPUT_DIR as fallback. Creates the directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Generates a filename by combining flow_name, context_name, and current timestamp from get_current_timestamp(), then sanitizes it using sanitize_filename() and appends '.png' extension. Constructs the full path by joining the directory and filename. Calls page.screenshot() with path parameter and full_page=True to capture the entire scrollable page. Returns the filename (not the full path). This is used for validation checkpoints to capture the complete page state.

functions.what_it_does: Applies color highlighting to validation result rows in an Excel worksheet based on Text_Result and Screenshot_Result column values. First extracts the header row to build a mapping of column names to indices. Defines a normalize function that converts cell values to uppercase stripped strings. Gets the normalized values of Text_Result and Screenshot_Result columns. If both are empty or N/A, returns without highlighting. Then applies complex conditional logic to determine the appropriate fill color from Constants: PASS (both pass), PASS_TEXT_ONLY (text pass, screenshot N/A), PASS_WITH_EXCLUDES (pass with exclusions), or FAIL (any failure). Finally applies the determined fill color to all cells in the row. This provides visual feedback on validation results in the Excel report.
functions.usage_example: highlight_validation(worksheet, row_cells)  # Highlights row based on validation results

parameters.description: Directory where report files should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Generates both JSON and HTML validation reports from a list of result dictionaries. Creates the output directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs(). Constructs file paths for JSON and HTML files by joining output_dir with filename and appropriate extensions. Writes the data list to a JSON file with indent=2 for readability using json.dump(). Then generates an HTML report by writing HTML structure with a table. If data is not empty, extracts headers from the first dictionary's keys and writes them as table headers. Iterates through each row dictionary and writes table rows with values for each header. Closes the HTML tags. This provides both machine-readable (JSON) and human-readable (HTML) validation reports.

functions.what_it_does: Writes validation or scraping data rows to a specific Excel worksheet with header management, duplicate removal, and optional row highlighting. Gets or creates the sheet by name. If the sheet is empty (max_row==0), writes the header row and applies header fill color from Constants.COLOR_HEADER. If the sheet has data, validates that the first row matches the expected header, and if not, deletes the first row and writes the correct header. Identifies and collects indices of empty rows and duplicate header rows for deletion. Deletes these rows in reverse order to maintain correct indices. Iterates through the provided rows, skipping empty rows. For each row, if it's a dictionary, extracts values in header order; otherwise uses the row as-is. Appends the row to the sheet. If highlight_func is provided, calls it with the worksheet, the newly added row cells, and None. Finally saves the workbook to the specified path.

Calls write_excel_context() with the provided results, workbook, sheet_name, and path parameters. Passes Constants.VALIDATION_HEADERS as the header parameter to ensure consistent column structure across all validation reports. Passes highlight_validation as the highlight_func parameter to automatically apply color coding to validation result rows based on pass/fail status. This simplifies the common use case of writing validation reports with standard headers and highlighting.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: CamelCase string to convert

functions.docstring:
Convert CamelCase flow names to snake_case module/function names.
e.g. CreateV3Account -> create_v3_account
functions.what_it_does: Converts CamelCase strings to snake_case format for Python naming conventions. Uses two regular expression substitutions. First, re.sub() with pattern '(.)([A-Z][a-z]+)' inserts an underscore between a lowercase letter and an uppercase letter followed by lowercase letters (e.g., 'Create' -> 'Create_'). Second substitution with pattern '([a-z0-9])([A-Z])' inserts an underscore between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter (e.g., 'V3Account' -> 'V3_Account'). Finally converts the entire string to lowercase using lower(). Returns the snake_case string. This is used to convert flow class names like 'CreateV3Account' to module names like 'create_v3_account'.

functions.what_it_does: Extracts and returns the language code from flow arguments dictionary. Uses dict.get() to retrieve the value associated with the key 'tenant_country_language_code' from the flow_args dictionary. Returns the language code string if present, or None if the key does not exist. This is a simple accessor function used throughout the framework to get the language/locale code for test execution.

functions.docstring: Get the target directory name based on target and simulator_platform parameters from flow args.

Defines a comprehensive mapping dictionary target_folder_mapping that maps various platform identifiers to standardized folder names (e.g., 'web_chrome' -> 'chrome', 'Android' -> 'android', 'GothamDesktop' -> 'wingotham', etc.). Checks if the target parameter starts with 'web_' using startswith(). If true, looks up the target in the mapping and returns the mapped value or the original target if not found. If target does not start with 'web_', looks up the simulator_platform in the mapping and returns the mapped value or the original target if not found. This standardizes directory naming across different platform configurations for organizing test outputs.

functions.method_signature: def run_flow(mode: str, flow_name: str, flow_args: Optional[Dict] = None, context_override: Optional[str] = None) -> None

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: Execution mode: 'scrape', 'validate', or 'noop'

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow to execute

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Optional dictionary of flow arguments and configuration

parameters.name: context_override
parameters.description: Optional context name override (not used in current implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Main orchestration function that executes a test flow in scrape, validate, or noop mode with comprehensive setup, execution, and reporting. Validates that flow_name is provided. Converts flow_name from CamelCase to snake_case to construct the module path. Dynamically imports the flow module using importlib.import_module(). Verifies the module contains the expected function. Generates a run_id timestamp and extracts language code, target directory, and other parameters from flow_args. Based on the mode parameter, sets up appropriate output directories and defines a stage_callback function that either calls scrape_context, validate_context, or does nothing (noop). Initializes Config with all flow arguments. Optionally initializes ReportsManager for HTML/JSON reporting if not in scrape mode. Executes the flow function within a try-except-finally block, passing the stage_callback. Handles exceptions by logging errors and setting flow_success to False. In the finally block, finalizes the report if it exists. After execution, checks for validation failures or interruptions and raises SystemExit(1) if found. Logs completion message with output directory path. This is the central flow execution engine for the entire framework.

functions.method_signature: def load_json_file(file_path: str, encoding: str = 'utf-8') -> dict

functions.what_it_does: Loads and parses a JSON file, returning the parsed data as a dictionary with comprehensive error handling. Wraps file operations in try-except blocks. Opens the file at file_path with the specified encoding using a context manager. Parses the JSON content using json.load() and returns the resulting dictionary. If the file is not found, raises FileNotFoundError with a descriptive message including the file path. If JSON parsing fails, catches json.JSONDecodeError and raises ValueError with a descriptive message including the file path and the original error. This provides safe JSON file loading with clear error messages for debugging.
functions.usage_example: data = load_json_file('config/settings.json')  # Returns parsed JSON as dict

functions.what_it_does: Loads locale mapping data from a JSON file specified in Constants.LOCALE_MAP_PATH. Simply calls load_json_file() with the path from Constants.LOCALE_MAP_PATH and returns the parsed dictionary. This provides a convenient way to access locale/language configuration data used throughout the framework for internationalization and localization testing.

Simply calls load_json_file() with the path from Constants.PRINTER_PROFILES_PATH and returns the parsed dictionary. This provides access to printer model configurations, SKUs, and capabilities used for printer-specific test scenarios.
functions.usage_example: printer_profiles = load_printer_profiles()  # Returns printer profiles dictionary

Then uses dict.get() to retrieve and return the address data for the specified country key. Returns the country-specific address dictionary if found, or None if the country is not in the data. This provides test data for address fields in country-specific formats.
functions.usage_example: us_address = load_address_for_country('United States')  # Returns US address data dict

functions.method_signature: def get_printer_details_for_country(profile_name: str, region: str, printer_profiles: dict, easy_enroll_flag: str = None, biz_model: str = 'Flex') -> tuple

parameters.description: Region code

parameters.description: Dictionary of printer profiles loaded from JSON
